answer:
**Supporting your friends' claim:**

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis," *Journal of Vocational Behavior*** — Across 92 studies, pay level correlated with job satisfaction at only r ≈ .15. People at very different salary levels report similar satisfaction, suggesting marginal salary bumps buy little happiness.

**Wrzesniewski, Schwartz et al. (2014), "Multiple Types of Motives Don't Multiply the Motivation of West Point Cadets," *PNAS*** — Tracking 11,000+ cadets over a decade, internal motives (genuine interest) predicted career success, while instrumental motives (pay, status) predicted worse outcomes. Strikingly, holding instrumental motives *alongside* internal ones actually diminished the benefit of internal motivation.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work," *Journal of Research in Personality*** — People who view work as a "calling" report substantially higher work and life satisfaction than those oriented toward pay ("job") or advancement ("career"). The orientation mattered more than income or occupation itself.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis," *Psychological Bulletin*** — Intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance, especially for quality-focused, complex work like research. However, extrinsic incentives also predict performance, so both jointly matter — "almost exclusively" overstates it.

**Complicating the claim:**

**Sheldon & Elliot (1999), "Goal Striving, Need Satisfaction, and Longitudinal Well-Being: The Self-Concordance Model," *JPSP*** — Pursuing goals aligned with one's authentic values and long-held aspirations predicts sustained well-being. Relevant nuance: leading a team is something *you've always wanted*, making that goal itself self-concordant, not merely "extrinsic."

**Johnston & Lee (2013), "Extra Status and Extra Stress? Are Promotions Good for Us?" *ILR Review*** — Promotions to managerial roles boost job satisfaction initially, but the effect fades within a few years and mental strain increases. Worth knowing before weighing the leadership opportunity too heavily — or too lightly.

**Frederick & Loewenstein (1999), "Hedonic Adaptation," in *Well-Being: The Foundations of Hedonic Psychology*** — People adapt rapidly and almost completely to income increases, but adapt less to ongoing daily experiences. Day-to-day engagement with your work tends to resist adaptation more than a salary bump does.

**Chen, Ellsworth & Schwarz (2015), "Finding a Fit or Developing It: Implicit Theories About Achieving Passion for Work," *PSPB*** — Passion for work can be *developed* over time, not just found pre-existing; "develop" theorists end up just as satisfied as "fit" theorists. **Killingsworth (2021), "Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year," *PNAS*** — Contra the famous plateau finding, real-time happiness continues rising log-linearly with income. Money isn't irrelevant — it's just that each additional dollar matters less.

**Bottom line:** the literature strongly supports weighting interestingness heavily — but "almost exclusively" is an overstatement, and your leadership aspiration is itself an intrinsic motive the research says you shouldn't dismiss.

answer:
Short answer: it's partially true — intrinsic interest is a strong predictor of satisfaction and performance, but the "almost exclusively" part isn't well supported. The literature also shows interest can grow in new roles, and needs like autonomy and growth (e.g., leading a team) matter a lot. Relevant papers:

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis"**
Across 86 studies, pay level correlates only weakly (~r = .15) with job satisfaction. This supports your friends' point that a moderately higher salary alone is unlikely to make you happier.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"**
People who experience their work as a "calling" report substantially higher life and work satisfaction than those who see it as a job (money) or career (advancement). Finding the work itself meaningful is one of the strongest predictors of well-being at work.

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "The 'What' and 'Why' of Goal Pursuits: Human Needs and the Self-Determination of Behavior"**
Well-being and sustained motivation depend on satisfying three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness — not interest alone. Notably, a leadership role you've always wanted could satisfy these needs in ways interesting-but-stagnant work might not.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018), "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?"**
People who believe interests are "found" (fixed) lose motivation quickly when work gets hard, while those who believe interests are developed sustain engagement. This cuts against your friends' framing: the Google role might become interesting, especially since the team-leading part excites you.

**Gilbert & Wilson (2000), "Miswanting: Some Problems in the Forecasting of Future Affective States"**
People systematically mispredict how future choices will make them feel, overweighting salient features of the options. Your current assessment of "interesting" vs. "less interesting" may not track how you'll actually feel six months into either role.

**Vallerand et al. (2003), "Les Passions de l'Âme: On Obsessive and Harmonious Passion"**
Passion for an activity predicts well-being only when it's "harmonious" (freely integrated into life) rather than "obsessive." Maximizing interestingness exclusively can backfire if it crowds out other goals you care about — like leadership growth.

**Sheldon & Lyubomirsky (2012), "The Challenge of Staying Happier: Testing the Hedonic Adaptation Prevention Model"**
People adapt even to positive circumstances, but variety and novelty slow this adaptation. Worth noting both ways: you may be partially adapted to your current "interesting" work, and a novel challenge could provide a genuine well-being boost.

**Bottom line from the literature:** interest matters more than salary, but it's one of several core needs — and the desire to lead, which you describe as a long-held goal, maps onto autonomy and growth needs that research weighs heavily too. "Almost exclusively" overstates the evidence.

answer:
**Papers supporting your friends' view:**

**"The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature" (Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010, *Journal of Vocational Behavior*)**
A meta-analysis of 86 studies found the correlation between pay level and job satisfaction is surprisingly weak (r ≈ .15). This suggests salary differences matter far less for day-to-day satisfaction than people assume.

**"Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion" (Kahneman, Krueger, Schkade, Schwarz & Stone, 2006, *Science*)**
People systematically exaggerate how much income affects happiness because they focus on it while making comparisons, even though it barely influences moment-to-moment experience. The salary bump may loom larger in your decision than it will in your actual life.

People who view their work as a calling (intrinsically meaningful) report significantly higher work and life satisfaction than those who view it as a job (money) or career (advancement). The orientation toward work mattered more than the occupation or income itself.

**"Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being" (Ryan & Deci, 2000, *American Psychologist*)**

Day-to-day satisfaction is driven most by a sense of *progress* in meaningful work, not by how interesting the domain is in the abstract. A role where you progress toward a long-held goal (like leading a team) could outperform a more interesting role where you feel stagnant.

answer:
**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis" (Psychological Bulletin).** Intrinsic motivation is a strong and reliable predictor of performance, especially for quality of work, but extrinsic incentives matter too and the two operate jointly rather than one dominating. So "interest matters a lot" is supported; "almost exclusively" is not.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis" (Journal of Vocational Behavior).** Across 86 studies, pay level correlated only weakly with job satisfaction (r ≈ .15). **Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work" (Journal of Research in Personality).** People who see their work as a "calling" report higher life and job satisfaction than those oriented toward pay or advancement, even within the same occupation. **Bottom line:** the research supports your friends directionally — intrinsic interest and meaning predict satisfaction better than pay — but "almost exclusively" is an overstatement, since autonomy, sense of control, and growth (which leading a team may provide) are comparably strong predictors.

**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being" (*American Psychologist*)**

answer:

A meta-analysis of 92 samples found pay level correlates only weakly with job satisfaction (r ≈ .15) and pay satisfaction. People at very different salary levels report nearly identical job satisfaction, suggesting a raise alone is a weak reason to switch.

Analysis of ~12,000 diary entries from knowledge workers found the single biggest driver of positive inner work life was making progress on meaningful work. Managers consistently underestimated this factor, ranking it last among motivators — which supports the "underrated" claim.

**Honest synthesis:** the research supports your friends directionally — interest/meaning is a stronger and more underrated predictor of satisfaction than pay — but "almost exclusively" overstates it, and the leadership opportunity (autonomy, growth, a long-held goal) is itself a well-supported satisfaction driver, not just an extrinsic one. **Hsee, Zhang, Yu & Xi (2003), "Lay Rationalism and Inconsistency Between Predicted Experience and Decision"** — People systematically overweight "hard," quantifiable attributes like salary and underweight experiential attributes like how enjoyable the work feels when making choices. **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis"** — Across 92 studies, pay level correlated only about r = .15 with job satisfaction. Differences in pay explain remarkably little about how much people like their jobs.

(2014), "Multiple Types of Motives Don't Multiply the Motivation of West Point Cadets"** — In a sample of over 10,000 cadets, internal motives predicted long-term success, while adding instrumental motives (money, status) on top actually predicted *worse* outcomes. **Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being"** — Sustained motivation and well-being depend on autonomy, competence, and relatedness, not just task interest. **Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"** — People who view their work as a calling report significantly higher life and job satisfaction than those who view it as a job (money) or career (advancement). answer:
**Papers supporting your friends' view:**

**"Multiple types of motives don't multiply the motivation of West Point cadets" — Wrzesniewski, Schwartz, et al. (PNAS, 2014)**
Tracking 10,000+ cadets over a decade, internally motivated cadets (genuine interest in the work) had better career outcomes, while holding instrumental motives (money, status) alongside internal ones actually *worsened* outcomes. Suggests choosing primarily for salary/title can be counterproductive even when interest is also present.

**"A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation" — Deci, Koestner & Ryan (Psychological Bulletin, 1999)**
A meta-analysis of 128 experiments showing that extrinsic rewards can crowd out intrinsic motivation for interesting tasks. Implies that taking a less interesting job for more money may degrade your engagement over time.

**"Goal Striving, Need Satisfaction, and Longitudinal Well-Being: The Self-Concordance Model" — Sheldon & Elliot (JPSP, 1999)**
Goals pursued for intrinsic reasons get more sustained effort and produce more well-being upon attainment than goals pursued for external reasons. Achievement at a "should" job tends to feel emptier than achievement at a "want" job.

**Papers complicating your friends' view:**

Interest is substantially *developed* through engagement, not just discovered pre-formed; people who treat passions as fixed give up faster when work gets hard. **"The 'What' and 'Why' of Goal Pursuits: Human Needs and the Self-Determination of Behavior" — Deci & Ryan (Psychological Inquiry, 2000)**
Sustained motivation and well-being depend on three needs—autonomy, competence, and *relatedness/growth*—not interestingness alone. Leading a team can satisfy needs (autonomy, growth into a long-held aspiration) that interesting individual-contributor work doesn't.

**"Income and emotional well-being: A conflict resolved" — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (PNAS, 2023)**
For most people, day-to-day happiness keeps rising with income well past earlier proposed plateaus. Salary isn't irrelevant, though at Meta/Google RE compensation levels the marginal effect of "a bit higher" is likely small.

**"Do People Become Healthier After Being Promoted?" — Boyce & Oswald (Health Economics, 2012)**
Promotions into roles with more responsibility produced no improvement in well-being and a measurable *decline* in mental health from added strain. A caution that "leading a team" can feel different in practice than in aspiration.

**"Affective Forecasting" — Wilson & Gilbert (Advances in Experimental Social Psychology, 2003)**
People systematically mispredict how much specific job features will affect their future happiness (the "impact bias") and adapt faster than expected to both gains and losses. Both the excitement of the new title and the pain of less interesting work will likely fade more than you predict.

**Bottom line:** the literature strongly supports interest/intrinsic motivation as a top-tier factor, but not your friends' "almost exclusively" framing—interest is malleable, autonomy and growth matter independently, and a long-deferred aspiration to lead is itself an intrinsic motive, not a status grab.
